title: Roast potatoes
subtitle: Duck-fat, par-boiled, shaken for crisp edges.
excerpt: Floury potatoes par-boiled until their edges begin to fluff, shaken in the colander to roughen the surface, then roasted in smoking-hot duck fat until the outside shatters and the inside is fluffy. The definitive British Sunday-roast side.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 — roast potato method in the vegetable chapter. Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747), public domain (potatoes roasted in the dripping pan under the joint). The duck-fat technique is traditional English practice documented in twentieth-century British cookery writing.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Par-boil and shake the potatoes up to four hours ahead. Leave them on a tray in a single layer to steam-dry; the drier they are when they hit the hot fat the crisper the result. Heat the fat and finish in the oven to order.

content.text: The route to a proper roast potato is not complicated, but it requires following each step without shortcutting. Par-boil the floury potatoes until their edges soften visibly. Drain and shake (not stir) so the edges fluff and fray into the rough surface that crisps in the fat. Heat the duck fat in the pan until it hisses when a potato touches it. Turn each potato to coat. Leave them alone in the oven until the underside is set, then turn once.

Duck fat is the traditional choice because its high smoke point lets the oven run hot enough to blister the surface, and its flavour complements the starch. Goose fat works identically. Vegetable oil gives a clean, slightly lighter result and is the practical choice for a plant-based table. Lard works. Olive oil smokes at a lower temperature and makes a softer crust.

items.prepNote: Maris Piper, King Edward, or Desiree — peeled and cut into golf-ball-sized chunks
or goose fat; vegetable oil works too
plus more for the boiling water

content.text: The roasting pan must be large enough that the potatoes sit in a single layer with some breathing room between each. If they crowd each other, they steam instead of roast and the crust never forms. Use two pans if necessary.

Spoon {{duck-fat}} of duck fat into the roasting pan and put it in the oven at 220°C (200°C fan). The fat must be properly hot (shimmering and nearly smoking) before the potatoes go in. This takes about 15 minutes.

Put {{potato}} of peeled potato chunks into a large saucepan of well-salted cold water. Bring to the boil and simmer for 8 to 10 minutes until the edges have softened and look slightly translucent. The centres should still be firm — a skewer goes in with resistance. Drain through a colander, then shake the colander firmly four or five times so the cut edges fray and fluff. This roughened surface is what makes the crust.

Carefully add the fluffy potatoes to the hot fat — it will spit. Turn each one so every surface is coated. Add the optional {{garlic}} of unpeeled garlic cloves and the {{rosemary}} of rosemary sprigs if using. Return the pan to the oven. Roast for 25 minutes without touching.

At 25 minutes, check the underside of a potato: it should be deep golden-brown and pulling cleanly away from the pan. If it's still pale, give it five more minutes and check again. Once the underside is set, turn each potato to expose an uncooked face. Roast for another 20 to 25 minutes until all surfaces are golden and the crust crackles when pressed. Season with {{sea-salt-flakes}} of sea salt flakes straight from the oven. Serve immediately.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: The crust either sets or it doesn't — these are the reasons it doesn't.

items.symptom: Potatoes soft and pale, no crust
items.cause: Fat not hot enough when potatoes went in, or pan too crowded.
items.fix: Heat the fat for at least 15 minutes at 220°C. Single layer in the pan, not piled on each other.

items.symptom: Edges are fluffy but inside is still raw after roasting
items.fix: Par-boil for a full 8 to 10 minutes. The centres should be firm but not hard; the edges visibly softened.

items.symptom: Potatoes stick to the pan and tear when turned
items.cause: Turned too early, before the underside crust set.
items.fix: Wait a full 25 minutes before the first turn. If they still stick, leave another five minutes. A set crust releases cleanly.

items.cause: Too much fat, or potatoes not dried enough after par-boiling.
items.fix: Shake the potatoes dry in the colander until no steam rises. Use the given amount of fat — more doesn't help.

content.text: Roast potatoes are the non-negotiable accompaniment to the British Sunday roast: the one side that can't be skipped, the one component the whole table notices if it isn't right. Hannah Glasse's 1747 recipe placed potatoes in the dripping pan directly under the roasting joint to catch the fat, a version still worth trying when roasting a chicken. The fat choice shifted from dripping and lard to duck and goose fat in the twentieth century, mainly because those fats became commercially available in sealed tins. The technique (par-boil, shake, hot fat) has not changed.
